Q1: Yes (mostly) the deployment would ideally also cover peer support conversations since a lot of the online mental health work we focus on happens in OMHC settings rather than formal clinical ones. Peer support (specifically) has quite different communicative patterns compared to CBT style sessions. People are less structured, more informal, and turns are shorter with more emotional back and forth. Crisis intervention is also relevant and those exchanges can be very different in tone and intent from a standard therapy session. So the 12 labels in HOPE would cover a good base but might not fully capture what happens in peer support or crisis contexts.
Q2: Yes this is a real concern for our use case. The HOPE data comes from YouTube videos which are predominantly (not all) USA based sessions. Our target users include practitioners and clients from India and the broader South Asian context and the way sessions are conducted there can differ in terms of formality and how much the counselor leads the conversation. That said since the sessions in HOPE are in English and follow a general counseling format the core dialogue act categories should still apply reasonably well. The more culturally specific communicative norms does not hinder the the current dialogue act label set as it has a broad communicative spectrum.
Q3: Dialogue acts are fundamentally about driving the flow of conversation and the 12 labels in HOPE are designed with that purpose in mind. What the question points to around reflection strategies and empathic distinctions is a different attribute altogether and sits more in the space of therapeutic quality assessment rather than dialogue act classification. That said the contextual nuances within each act category can certainly vary depending on cultural, regional and other local factors and that is a valid area of exploration. Our taxonomy covers the spectrum it is intended for well and the finer grained attributes the question raises are equally interesting but are more the scope of other related lines of works.
Q4: The dialogue act labels in HOPE were annotated by people with expertise in psycholinguistics and the labels themselves are not clinical in nature so they are well suited to be annotated by general linguists too. That said having a psychological understanding of the counseling space does strengthen the annotation quality and further validation by psychologists had only made the benchmark stronger. As for the risk of incorrect predictions affecting practitioners, we think it is fairly minimal. The scope of dialogue act classification is limited to understanding conversation flow and trajectory and it does not make any clinical judgements. So even if a label is predicted incorrectly it does not carry the kind of risk that a clinical decision would.
